Singapore Employment Rate by Education Attainment Across Age & Gender

AAI1001 Team Project Proposal

Author

AAI1001 Team 32

Published

Invalid Date

Team Information

Name Student ID
Adheesh 2500670
Shannon 2403263
Thiha Thein 2502266
Jing Hong 2503049
Zafri 2403108

1. Introduction and Project Overview

1.1 The Story We’re Telling

Singapore’s workforce has undergone a profound educational transformation over the past three decades. University-educated workers now make up the majority of employed residents in the 25–44 age bands, yet public debate continues to ask whether the labour market has truly absorbed this surge in graduate supply — or whether a structural mismatch has quietly emerged between qualifications and available jobs.

Our project seeks to answer three interrelated questions using official 2024 Ministry of Manpower data.

First, how does educational attainment among employed Singaporeans actually vary across age groups — and does the composition differ meaningfully from what population-level data alone suggests?

Second, are there meaningful gender gaps in the education–employment relationship that aggregate statistics conceal? The original visualization hints at gender differences but its crowded layout makes them impossible to quantify.

Third, does employment rate data by age and sex support or challenge the narrative that younger, highly educated cohorts face disproportionate labour market difficulty?

Our improved chart aims answers all three questions in a single view by encoding four dimensions simultaneously: age group, sex, employment rate, and education composition. The redesign moves beyond the original chart’s population-count framing to focus on the employed resident workforce, revealing where educational capital is concentrated, where gender gaps are widest, and which cohorts — if any — show signs of underemployment relative to their qualifications.

1.2 Source & Context

The original visualization was published by FYT Consultants in a blog post titled “A Data-Driven Look at Singapore’s Graduate Unemployment Debate” (September 2025), available at:

https://www.fytconsultants.com/single-post/a-data-driven-look-at-singapore-s-graduate-unemployment-debate

The underlying data comes from the Singapore Department of Statistics (SingStat), specifically the annual dataset: Singapore Residents Aged 25 Years and Over by Highest Qualification Attained (HQA), Sex and Age Group.

1.2 Our Chosen Visualization

Figure 1: Original Tableau Visualization — "Population by Education and Gender Over Time – 2024"
Source: FYT Consultants / Singapore Department of Statistics

1.2.1 Visualization Structure and Design

The visualization selected is a dual-panel stacked bar chart titled “Population by Education and Gender Over Time – 2024”. The bar chart illustrates Singapore’s resident population aged 25 and above, broken down simultaneously across three dimensions:

  1. Age group — From ages 25–29 years old through to 65 years old & over
  2. Gender — Females and Males displayed as adjacent bar pairs within each age group
  3. Highest Educational Attainment — University, Diploma & Professional Qualification, Post Secondary (Non-Tertiary), Secondary, and Below Secondary

The chart comprises two vertically stacked panels:

  • Top panel (proportional / 100% stacked bars): Shows the percentage share of each education level within a given age–gender group, enabling comparison of educational composition across cohorts.
  • Bottom panel (absolute count bars): Shows the actual number of residents in each age–gender group, providing population size context that the proportional view alone cannot convey.

1.2.2 The Story It Tells

The visualization narrates a generational shift in educational attainment in Singapore. Several narratives emerge when analysing the stacked bar chart:

Younger cohorts are markedly more educated. In the 25–34 age bands, University (dark navy) dominates the top proportional panel, exceeding 50% for both males and females. This proportion of university graduates decreases dramatically as the age group increases.

Older cohorts show high “Below Secondary” and “Secondary” attainment. The Below Secondary (yellow) education level becomes prominent in the 60–64 and 65+ groups, reflecting limited access to higher levels of education in the earlier decades.

The 65+ age group is by far the largest in absolute size. The bottom panel reveals a striking spike in the 65+ bars compared to all other age groups, reflecting Singapore’s ageing population and contextualising the large “Below Secondary” proportion in that cohort.

Gender differences in educational attainment are visible but subtle. In younger cohorts, females and males show comparable university rates; in older cohorts, males sometimes show slightly higher university proportions, reflecting historical gender gaps in access to education.

The bar chart’s dual-panel design is the most distinctive structural feature — it attempts to showcase two stories at once: the composition story (top chart) and the population size story (bottom chart).

2. Critical Analysis of Original Visualization

Strengths

The original visualizaiton demonstrates several effective design choices:

#1. Dual-panel design provides complementary context

The most significant design strength is the pairing of a 100% stacked bar (proportional) with an absolute count bar beneath it. A proportional chart alone can be misleading — a cohort with 80% university graduates might represent only a tiny absolute population. Thus, the bottom panel mitigates misinterpretation by revealing the raw counts, making the 65+ spike immediately apparent. This design decision showcases awareness of a well-known pitfall in compositional visualization.

#2. Consistent colour encoding across education levels

The 5 education levels are mapped to a sequential–diverging palette, with a legend attached for easy referencing. The dark navy (University) and medium blue (Diploma) occupy the “cool” end of the spectrum, while the yellow-green (Below Secondary) anchors the “warm” end. The colour logic is intuitive — darker/cooler tones are associated with higher attainment — making readability efficient.

#3. Multi-variable analysis in a single chart

The chart successfully visualizes the intersectional characteristics of age, gender, and education in a single view from a genuinely complex multidimensional dataset. Alternative approaches such as faceted line charts or heat maps would require the viewer to toggle between multiple panels to perceive these relationships together.

#4. Clear legend and descriptive subtitle

The legend is well-labelled, easy to understand, and directly matches the colour categories used in the bars. The subtitle explains what is being shown in both panels, which is necessary given the chart’s complexity.

Weaknesses

Despite its strengths, the original visualzization also has significant limitations that reduce its analytical value:

#1. Poor readability from severe x-axis label crowding

The age group labels are printed vertically above the top panel, while the gender labels (“Female” and “Male”) are rotated vertically below the bottom panel. With 9 age groups × 2 genders = 18 bar pairs, the labels overlap and are difficult to read at normal resolution. The viewer must invest significant effort tracking which bar belongs to which age group.

#2. Visual discontinuity between the two panels

The top and bottom panels are not visually connected by gridlines, reference lines, or shading. The viewer must mentally align bars across the two panels to link a proportional observation with its absolute count. The lack of a linking visual device weakens the narrative connection that is the chart’s core strength.

#3. Stacked bars make mid-segment comparison almost impossible

Since “Below Secondary” is the only category anchored to a common baseline (0%), all other education levels float at varying heights. Comparing the “Post Secondary (Non-Tertiary)” share between, say, 35–39 year-old females and 55–59 year-old males requires estimating floating segment boundaries across two different bars — a task that is cognitively very demanding.

#4. The 65+ absolute bar dwarfs all other bars, distorting the bottom panel

The 65+ cohort is so much larger (400K+) that all other bars in the bottom panel appear compressed. The y-axis scale accommodates the maximum value, reducing comparative resolution across all other age groups.

#5. No direct value annotations

Neither panel includes data labels or tooltips, as the visualization is a static export. The viewer cannot determine precise percentages or counts without a reference grid.

#6. Colour palette has accessibility concerns

The distinction between the light blue (Diploma & Professional Qualification) and light green (Post Secondary Non-Tertiary) segments is subtle and may be difficult for viewers with colour vision deficiencies.

3. Proposed Improvements

3.1 Enhancement Strategy

Based on the six weaknesses identified in Section 2, we propose six concrete, targeted improvements. The table below maps each improvement to the weakness it directly addresses, and notes which original strengths are preserved.

# Weakness Addressed Improvement Method / Technique
I1 W1 Replace rotated age labels with a centred spine column between the two wings. Every label is horizontal, legible, and visually anchored between its male and female bars — the butterfly spine eliminates the 18-label crowding entirely. Flexbox butterfly layout; fixed-width centre column
I2 W2 W3 Merge the two panels into one dual-encoded bar: width encodes employment rate (%), fill encodes education composition (%). Dual encoding eliminates panel discontinuity (W2) and anchors all segments to a consistent proportional baseline (W3). Dual-encoding on a single bar axis
I3 W3 Segment visibility toggle — clicking any education category recalculates the remaining proportions to sum to 100%, enabling direct comparison of selected categories on a consistent scale without floating baselines. Interactive segment filter; live proportion recalculation
I4 W4 Encode employment rate as bar width (0–100%) rather than absolute count. The 65+ and 70+ cohorts naturally produce shorter bars due to lower participation, eliminating the axis distortion caused by the 65+ population spike. Rate-normalised bar width instead of absolute count axis
I5 W5 Employment rate printed directly as a pill label at each bar end. Hovering reveals a tooltip with the exact rate and all active education breakdowns to 2 d.p., recalculated for visible segments — surfacing values that were invisible in the static original. Inline bar-end pill labels; floating rich tooltip on hover
I6 W6 Replace the original palette with the Wong (2011) colourblind-safe set: Dark Gray, Blue, Orange, Purple, Green — each pair maintaining sufficient luminance contrast under deuteranopia and protanopia. Segment toggle further reduces colour dependency. Wong (2011) CB-safe palette; interactive segment isolation
✅ Original Strengths Preserved
S1 Gender comparison retained. The butterfly layout keeps males and females in direct visual opposition about a shared age spine — the core comparative intent of the original is maintained, and actually strengthened by eliminating label clutter.
S2 Colour encoding consistent. Education levels retain a consistent colour identity across all bars. The Wong palette replaces the original while preserving the principle of one colour per education category, with the added benefit of colourblind accessibility.
S3 Single multi-variable view. Age, gender, employment rate, and education composition are all visible simultaneously in one chart — no panel-switching required. The redesign adds a fourth dimension (employment rate) while keeping the single-view principle intact.

3.2 Justification for Redesign Approach

Rather than reconstructing the original Tableau chart identically, we redesign it as a fully interactive HTML visualization. Three core principles underpin this decision:

🔄
Interactivity Resolves Structural Weaknesses
The original is a static image. As identified in W3 and W5, the inability to isolate segments or surface exact values is a structural limitation of the static format — not a design choice. An interactive redesign resolves these directly: segment toggles anchor floating comparisons, and hover tooltips surface precise values on demand.
📐
Dual Encoding Adds a Labour Market Dimension
The original encodes only education composition. Our redesign layers in employment rates from MOM Table B1, adding a critical labour-market dimension. Bar width now encodes participation while fill encodes composition — simultaneously answering "who is employed?" and "how educated are they?" without requiring a second panel.
💡
Self-Annotating Chart Integrates Explanation
Controls, tooltips, and inline bar-end labels replace the need for a separate narrative. The chart is self-annotating — a reader can extract precise values, filter by cohort, and isolate education segments without leaving the visual. This fulfils both the "effective communication" and "aesthetically pleasing and professional" criteria simultaneously.
🔍 Design Principle The butterfly (diverging bar) layout eliminates the 18-label crowding problem while preserving the gender comparison that was the core intent of the original. The mirror-image structure is perceptually natural — viewers read left as one group and right as another, with the central age spine as the reference point.

3.3 Proposed Visualization Design

3.3.1 Interactive Butterfly Chart — Employment Rate by Age, Gender & Education Level (2024)

The visualization below encodes four dimensions simultaneously: age group, gender, employment rate, and education level share. Hovering over any bar reveals the exact employment rate and the precise percentage breakdown across visible education categories for that age × gender group.

👥Age Group (y-axis, 12 groups)
⚧Gender (left / right wings)
📊Employment Rate % (bar width)
🎓Education Share % (stacked fill colour)
Show segments:
Filter ages:
Select age groups
Male
Age
Female
Employment Rate (%)

Source: MOM Comprehensive Labour Force Survey 2024 · Table B1 (employment rates by age and sex) · Table G4 (education counts by age and sex) · Manpower Research & Statistics Department, Ministry of Manpower, Singapore.

3.3.2 Key Design Decisions

  • 1
    Diverging butterfly layout I1 → W1
    The mirror-image horizontal bar structure places male and female bars about a central age spine, eliminating the 18-label crowding of the original entirely. Every label is horizontal, legible, and unambiguously linked to its bars.
  • 2
    Dual encoding: width × fill I2 → W2 + W3
    Bar width maps to employment rate (0–100%) while bar fill segments map to education composition. This merges the original's two disconnected panels into one view — simultaneously eliminating panel discontinuity and anchoring all segments to a consistent baseline.
  • 3
    Live proportion recalculation on segment toggle I3 → W3
    Deselecting any education category removes it from the chart and redistributes proportions so the remaining segments always sum to 100%. This ensures the stacked fill remains visually coherent and percentages in the tooltip reflect only the visible categories.
  • 4
    Rate-normalised bar width I4 → W4
    By using employment rate (%) rather than absolute count as bar width, all 12 age groups are placed on the same 0–100% scale. The 65+ and 70+ cohorts naturally produce shorter bars, eliminating the axis distortion caused by the 65+ population spike.
  • 5
    Prominent pill labels + rich tooltip I5 → W5
    Employment rates are displayed as bold pill labels at each bar end for immediate readability. Hovering reveals the full education breakdown, recalculated for currently active segments, surfacing values that were entirely invisible in the static original.
  • 6
    Wong (2011) colourblind-safe palette I6 → W6
    Five education categories mapped to Dark Gray, Blue, Orange, Purple, and Green — each pair maintaining sufficient luminance contrast under deuteranopia and protanopia simulation. The segment toggle further reduces dependence on colour alone.

3.3.3 Interaction Features

The visualization supports three interactive features, each resolving a specific analytical limitation of the static original:

🎛️ Segment Toggle

Click any education category to remove it from view. Remaining segments recalculate to sum to 100%, enabling clean comparison of any subset of education levels with a consistent proportional scale.

🔦 Multi-Select Age Filter

A dropdown panel lets users check or uncheck any combination of age groups. Deselected rows are removed completely from the chart — not merely dimmed — keeping the view uncluttered.

💬 Rich Tooltip on Hover

Hovering any bar reveals the employment rate and the recalculated percentage split across all currently active education categories (2 d.p., summing to 100%), keeping the tooltip consistent with what is visible in the chart.

4. Data Sources & Data Quality

4.1 Selected Datasets

Our improved butterfly visualization requires two datasets from the Ministry of Manpower (MOM) Comprehensive Labour Force Survey 2024, both sourced from the MOM Statistics portal. Both are contained within the official published workbooks and are used exclusively for the year 2024.

Primary Dataset
Table B1 — Resident Employment Rate by Age and Sex, 2014–2024
File: LFR2024_SectionB_EmploymentRate.xlsx, Sheet B1
Publisher: Manpower Research & Statistics Dept, MOM Singapore
Unit: Per cent (%)
Coverage: 12 age groups × 3 sex categories (Total, Male, Female) × 11 years
We use: Male and Female rows, year 2024 column only
employment rate age group sex 2024
Secondary Dataset
Table G4 — Employed Residents by Highest Qualification, Age and Sex, 2024
File: LFR2024_SectionG_MedianAgeDemographicProfile.xlsx, Sheet G4
Publisher: Manpower Research & Statistics Dept, MOM Singapore
Unit: Thousands of employed residents
Coverage: 12 age groups × 3 sex categories × 5 education levels
We use: Male and Female rows, all 5 education level columns, 2024 only
education level age group sex count (thousands)

4.2 Justification for Dataset Selection

These two datasets were chosen over all other available MOM tables for three specific reasons that directly serve the improved visualization design:

B1 resolves the 65+ distortion problem (W4). The original visualization used absolute resident population counts as bar lengths, which caused the 65+ cohort to dwarf all other groups. By using employment rates from B1 as bar width instead, all 12 age groups are placed on a uniform 0–100% scale. The 65+ and 70+ cohorts naturally produce shorter bars that reflect their lower labour market participation, without any axis distortion.

G4 provides the education composition for employed residents specifically. The original chart used all residents (employed and non-employed) from SingStat population data. Our redesign focuses the education breakdown on employed residents only — the population actually participating in the labour market. This is a more analytically meaningful lens and directly justifies why B1 and G4 must come from the same MOM Labour Force Survey rather than from separate sources.

Together they enable dual encoding across four dimensions. No single table alone supports the butterfly chart’s design. Joining B1 (employment rates) with G4 (education composition counts) creates the combined structure needed to encode age, sex, employment rate, and education simultaneously in one visualization.

Minimum Dimension Requirement The project requires at least 4 dimensions in the final visualization. The joined dataset encodes 4 dimensions simultaneously: Age Group (y-axis), Sex (left/right butterfly wings), Employment Rate % (bar width), and Education Level (stacked fill colour). This satisfies the requirement within a single chart without a secondary panel.

4.3 Raw Data Structure and Known Challenges

4.3.1 B1 Raw Layout

The B1 sheet does not follow a simple rectangular table structure. It uses a multi-block layout where each sex group (Total, Male, Female) occupies a separate vertical block of rows, preceded by several metadata rows. The structure is as follows:

Row 1       : "Contents"           ← sheet navigation label, skip
Row 2       : "TABLE B1"           ← title, skip
Row 3       : long title string    ← skip
Rows 4–6    : blank / units row    ← skip
Row 7       : [NULL, NULL, 2014, 2015, ..., 2024]   ← year headers
Row 8       : "Total", "Total", 64.5, ...            ← Total block header
Rows 9–19   : NULL, "15 - 19" ... "70 & Over"       ← 12 age rows (Total)
Rows 20–25  : blank + sub-aggregates                 ← skip
Row 27      : "Male", "Total", 73.3, ...             ← Male block header
Rows 28–38  : NULL, "15 - 19" ... "70 & Over"       ← 12 age rows (Male)
Rows 39–45  : blank + sub-aggregates                 ← skip
Row 46      : "Female", "Total", 56.2, ...           ← Female block header
Rows 47–57  : NULL, "15 - 19" ... "70 & Over"       ← 12 age rows (Female)

The year 2024 occupies column 13 (columns 1–2 are labels; column 3 is 2014, incrementing one column per year).

4.3.2 G4 Raw Layout

G4 follows the same multi-block convention but has an additional complexity: the education levels appear as column headers (not rows), making it a two-dimensional table within each sex block.

Row 1       : "Contents"           ← skip
Row 2       : "TABLE G4"           ← skip
Row 3       : long title string    ← skip  
Rows 4–6    : blank / units row    ← skip
Row 7       : ["", NULL, "Total", "Below Secondary", "Secondary",
               "Post-Secondary (Non-Tertiary)",
               "Diploma & Professional Qualification", "Degree"]
Row 8       : "Total", "Total", 2365.6, 271.7, ...   ← Both sexes total block
Rows 9–19   : NULL, "15 - 19" ... "70 & Over"        ← 12 age rows (Both)
Row 20      : blank separator
Row 21      : "Male", "Total", 1238.0, 150.2, ...    ← Male block header
Rows 22–32  : NULL, "15 - 19" ... "70 & Over"        ← 12 age rows (Male)
Row 33      : blank separator
Row 34      : "Female", "Total", 1127.6, 121.5, ...  ← Female block header
Rows 35–45  : NULL, "15 - 19" ... "70 & Over"        ← 12 age rows (Female)

4.3.3 Identified Data Quality Challenges

The table below catalogues every known structural issue in the raw files and assigns a severity rating based on how likely it is to cause silent data errors if not handled correctly.

ID File / Sheet Challenge Severity Planned Resolution
C1 Both files Multiple metadata header rows precede the data table. Reading the file naively (e.g. with default read_excel()) will consume header rows as data rows. High Use col_names = FALSE on ingestion to preserve all rows, then extract data using fixed row offsets derived from visual inspection and documented as named constants in the code.
C2 Both files Data is split across three vertical blocks (Total / Male / Female) within a single sheet. There are no column markers indicating which block a row belongs to — only positional structure. High Extract each gender block independently using its known start row. Male and Female start rows are stored as named constants (B1_MALE_START, G4_FEMALE_START, etc.) so the logic is transparent and auditable.
C3 G4 Negligible or suppressed counts are represented as the string "-" rather than 0 or NA. This occurs for cells such as the 15–19 Degree group where the count is effectively zero. Calling as.numeric() on "-" produces NA, which would silently corrupt proportion calculations. High Explicitly test for "-" before numeric coercion and replace with 0. This is the statistically correct interpretation — the count is not missing, it is zero or suppressed due to rounding.
C4 G4 Counts are published as rounded thousands (e.g. 132.3 means 132,300 people). Because of this rounding, the five education-level counts within an age × sex group will not sum to exactly the published total. Proportion sums will therefore deviate slightly from 1.0. Medium Compute proportions as count / total using the published total as the denominator. Accept deviations within ±2% as a known rounding artefact. Validate and flag in code comments. Units (thousands) will be retained throughout — no multiplication to raw counts needed.
C5 B1 The sheet contains sub-aggregate rows (e.g. "15–24", "25–64", "55–64") interleaved between the 12 individual age-group rows and the next sex block. These could be accidentally included in extraction if row offsets are miscounted. Medium Extract exactly 12 rows starting from the documented start row for each sex block. The 12 individual age groups immediately follow the block header row with no interruption. A spot-check assertion will confirm that the 12th extracted row corresponds to "70 & Over".
C6 Both files R's read_excel() assigns generic column names (...1, ...2, etc.) when col_names = FALSE is used. Columns must be referenced by numeric index rather than name, which can be brittle if the sheet layout shifts in a future MOM release. Low Reference columns by numeric index constants only (B1_COL_2024 <- 13L, G4_COL_TOTAL <- 3L, etc.). All index constants are defined together in a single named-constants block at the start of the pipeline and are independently verifiable against the raw sheet.

5. Data Engineering Workflow

5.1 Planned Data Engineering Pipeline

The pipeline proceeds through five sequential stages. Each stage has a clearly defined input, a set of operations, and a defined output that feeds the next stage.

Pipeline Principle Each stage is implemented as a named R function or a clearly commented code block. No stage modifies a previous stage's output in-place — transformations always produce new objects. This ensures every intermediate state is inspectable and the pipeline can be re-run cleanly from any stage.
1
Ingest Raw Excel Files
Both .xlsx workbooks will be loaded using readxl::read_excel() with col_names = FALSE and no row skipping, so that all rows — including metadata headers — are preserved as data rows. This gives us full control over which rows are treated as headers versus data. The readxl package is used specifically because it does not require a Java runtime, unlike the xlsx package, making the pipeline more portable across team members' machines. Sheets are specified by name ("B1", "G4") rather than position to guard against sheet order changes in future MOM releases.
readxl::read_excel() col_names = FALSE sheet = "B1" / "G4"
2
Extract 2024 Cross-Section by Gender Block
Using the row offset constants documented in §5.2, each gender block (Male, Female) is extracted from both sheets independently by slicing exactly 12 consecutive rows — one per age group. For B1, column 13 (year 2024) is extracted from each block. For G4, the total column and the five education-level columns are extracted from each block. The result of this stage is four raw tibbles: emp_male, emp_female, edu_male, edu_female. A spot-check will confirm that the extracted Male 30–34 employment rate matches the published value of 93.3%.
row slicing [start:(start+11)] column indexing by constant as.numeric() coercion "-" → 0 for G4
3
Reshape to Long (Tidy) Format and Standardise Labels
The four extracted tibbles are combined using dplyr::bind_rows() — first emp_male and emp_female into emp_2024 (24 rows), then edu_male and edu_female into edu_2024 (120 rows). Column names are assigned explicitly as part of the extraction step: sex, age, emp_rate for the employment table; sex, age, education, count_k, total_k for the education table. Age group and education level columns are converted to ordered factor types with levels defined by constants, which guarantees consistent ordering throughout all downstream transformations and chart rendering.
dplyr::bind_rows() factor(..., levels = AGE_LABELS) factor(..., levels = EDU_LABELS) explicit column naming
4
Derive Proportions, Bar Extents, and Butterfly Coordinates
This is the core feature engineering stage. Three new columns are derived. First, education proportion (prop) is computed as count_k / total_k within each age × sex group using dplyr::group_by() + mutate(). Second, bar extent (bar_extent) is computed as emp_rate × prop — this is the dual-encoding that maps employment rate to bar length and education composition to fill colour simultaneously. Third, cumulative stacking coordinates (xmin2, xmax2) are computed using cumsum() within each group, then sign-flipped for the Male wing so that male bars extend leftward (negative x-axis) to create the butterfly layout.
prop = count_k / total_k bar_extent = emp_rate × prop cumsum() for stacking sign flip for male wing
5
Join, Validate, and Export Visualization-Ready Objects
The education long table and the employment rate table are joined on sex and age using dplyr::left_join(), with the education table as the left (primary) side. After joining, four automated validation checks will run: (1) a completeness check that the joined table has exactly 120 rows; (2) a zero-NA check across all visualization-critical columns; (3) a proportion-sum check that all age × sex groups sum within ±2% of 1.0; and (4) a plausibility check that prime working-age (35–54) employment rates exceed 75% for both sexes. Two final objects are exported for the visualization section: employment_data (120 rows, the main chart data) and emp_pills (24 rows, bar-end label data).
left_join(by = c("sex","age")) completeness assertion NA check proportion-sum check plausibility stopifnot()

5.2 Planned Cleaning and Transformation Decisions

Every cleaning choice is listed below with an explicit rationale. This ensures that no transformation is implicit or invisible to future readers of the code.

# Issue Decision Rationale
D1 Metadata header rows above data Skip using fixed row offset constants; never use skip = argument in read_excel() skip = silently discards rows; constants are self-documenting and verifiable against the raw sheet
D2 "-" string in G4 education counts Replace with 0 before numeric coercion Statistically correct — these are suppressed near-zero counts, not missing values; avoids silent NA propagation into proportion calculations
D3 Counts published as rounded thousands Retain in thousands; compute proportions from published totals Multiplying to raw counts introduces false precision; thousands are sufficient for proportions and the chart does not show absolute counts
D4 Wide format (education as columns) Reshape to long within the extraction helper function Long format is required by ggplot2’s geom_rect() for stacked bar rendering; one row per sex × age × education
D5 No common baseline for education stacking Pre-compute cumulative xmin/xmax positions before plotting Passing raw proportions to position_stack in ggplot2 does not support the sign-flip required for the butterfly layout; pre-computation gives full control
D6 Male bars must mirror to negative x-axis Apply sign flip after cumulative sum: xmin2 = -xmax, xmax2 = -xmin Separates the stacking arithmetic cleanly from the butterfly layout logic; the stacking step is sex-agnostic, the flip step handles the visual layout
D7 Unordered factor levels would scramble stacking Convert age and education to ordered factors at reshape stage ggplot2 respects factor levels for ordering; declaring levels once at the reshape stage guarantees consistent order in all downstream objects

5.3 Target Output Schema

Two objects will be passed from the data engineering pipeline to the visualization section. Their schemas are defined here so that the visualization code can be written against a known, stable interface.

employment_data  ·  120 rows
age factor (12 levels, ordered)
sex factor (Male / Female)
education factor (5 levels, ordered)
emp_rate numeric — employment rate %
prop numeric — education share (0–1)
count_k numeric — employed residents (000s)
total_k numeric — total in age × sex group (000s)
bar_extentnumeric — emp_rate × prop
xmin2 numeric — segment left edge
xmax2 numeric — segment right edge
Primary chart data. One row per age × sex × education combination. xmin2 and xmax2 are pre-computed for geom_rect(); negative values indicate male wing.
emp_pills  ·  24 rows
age factor (12 levels, ordered)
sex factor (Male / Female)
emp_rate numeric — employment rate %
bar_end numeric — signed bar-end x position
Label data for employment rate pill annotations at each bar end. One row per age × sex group. bar_end is negative for males, positive for females.
Four Dimensions Confirmed The employment_data schema encodes exactly four dimensions used by the butterfly chart: age (y-axis), sex (left/right wings via sign of xmin2/xmax2), emp_rate (total bar width), and education (stacked fill segments). A fifth dimension — count_k — is available for tooltip display. This satisfies and exceeds the project's 4-dimension minimum requirement.

5.4 Illustrative Code Structure

The final pipeline code will follow the structure below. This is not the complete working code — full annotated R code will be provided in the final submission — but it demonstrates the planned function signatures and data flow.

# ── STAGE 1: Ingest ────────────────────────────────────────────
raw_b1 <- read_excel(path_b, sheet = "B1", col_names = FALSE)
raw_g4 <- read_excel(path_g, sheet = "G4", col_names = FALSE)

# ── STAGE 2: Extract 2024 cross-section ────────────────────────
# A named helper function per table type:
extract_emp_rates()    # args: raw sheet, start row, sex label, col index
extract_edu_counts()   # args: raw sheet, start row, sex label, col indices

emp_2024 <- bind_rows(
  extract_emp_rates(raw_b1, B1_MALE_START,   "Male",   B1_COL_2024),
  extract_emp_rates(raw_b1, B1_FEMALE_START, "Female", B1_COL_2024)
)

edu_2024 <- bind_rows(
  extract_edu_counts(raw_g4, G4_MALE_START,   "Male",   ...),
  extract_edu_counts(raw_g4, G4_FEMALE_START, "Female", ...)
)

# ── STAGE 3: Reshape and label ──────────────────────────────────
# Factor levels applied at this stage — once, and only here
edu_2024 <- edu_2024 |>
  mutate(
    age       = factor(age,       levels = AGE_LABELS),
    education = factor(education, levels = EDU_LABELS)
  )

# ── STAGE 4: Feature engineering ───────────────────────────────
edu_2024 <- edu_2024 |>
  group_by(sex, age) |>
  mutate(prop = count_k / total_k)   # education share within group

viz_long <- edu_2024 |>
  left_join(emp_2024, by = c("sex", "age")) |>
  mutate(bar_extent = emp_rate * prop) |>
  group_by(sex, age) |>
  mutate(
    xmax  = cumsum(bar_extent),
    xmin  = xmax - bar_extent,
    xmin2 = if_else(sex == "Male", -xmax, xmin),   # butterfly flip
    xmax2 = if_else(sex == "Male", -xmin, xmax)
  )

# ── STAGE 5: Validate and export ───────────────────────────────
stopifnot(nrow(viz_long) == 120)          # completeness
stopifnot(sum(is.na(viz_long)) == 0)      # no NAs
# ... proportion-sum and plausibility checks

employment_data    <- viz_long |> select(age, sex, education, emp_rate,
                                    prop, count_k, total_k, xmin2, xmax2)
emp_pills    <- ...   # 24-row summary for bar-end labels
Note on Code Status The pseudocode above illustrates the intended function signatures and data flow. The complete, fully annotated, and tested R implementation — with all validation outputs and intermediate data previews — will be provided in the final Week 13 submission.

5.5 Reproducibility Considerations

📁
File Structure
Both .xlsx source files will be stored in a datasets/ subfolder within the project root. File paths will be resolved programmatically relative to the .qmd document's own directory using knitr::current_input(), so no absolute paths are hard-coded. The pipeline will work on any team member's machine without path editing.
📦
Package Management
All required packages (readxl, dplyr, tidyr, purrr, ggplot2, scales) are loaded at the top of the pipeline section in a single library() block. Package versions will be captured and displayed using sessionInfo() at the end of the section, enabling any reviewer to verify the exact environment used for the final submission.
🔒
Source File Integrity
MD5 checksums of both source .xlsx files will be recorded using tools::md5sum() and printed within the document. This allows any reader to verify that the files used to produce the outputs are the original unmodified MOM published files, and provides a clear signal if files are ever accidentally replaced or updated.
📌
Explicit Constants
All row offsets and column indices derived from visual inspection of the raw sheets are stored as named constants (e.g. B1_MALE_START <- 28L, G4_COL_TOTAL <- 3L) in a single dedicated code block. No magic numbers appear inside extraction functions. If MOM updates the file layout, only the constants block needs updating — the extraction logic is unaffected.
✅
Built-in Assertions
Four automated validation checks (stopifnot() assertions) will be embedded directly in the pipeline: row count completeness, zero-NA check, proportion-sum tolerance, and prime-age plausibility. If any check fails, the document will halt with an informative error message rather than silently producing incorrect output. This makes data errors impossible to overlook during rendering.
🔄
No External Dependencies
The pipeline requires no internet connection, no database connections, and no API calls. All data is loaded from local files bundled with the project. The Quarto document can be rendered offline on any machine with R and the listed packages installed. There are no stochastic operations, so no random seed is needed — the output is fully deterministic.

6. Data Analysis Plan

Using the cleaned data and improved visualizations, we derive three key analytical insights:

Insight 1 — The graduate majority tipping point. Among residents aged 25–34, university graduates became the majority around 2013–2015. This is a cohort-level phenomenon: those born from the late 1980s onward entered a university system that had dramatically expanded capacity. By 2024, over 60% of residents in the 30–44 age bands hold degrees — the highest in Singapore’s history.

Insight 2 — Females are outpacing males in educational attainment. In the 30–34 age group, female university graduates number 100,500 vs 81,200 for males — a gap of nearly 20,000. Female university attainment (65.9%) exceeds male (57.7%) by 8 percentage points. This gender reversal in educational outcomes is invisible in the original visualization’s cluttered layout but has significant implications for workforce policy.

Insight 3 — Labour force degree holders nearly doubled in 14 years. Degree holders in the resident labour force grew from 565,000 (2010) to 1,061,000 (2024) — an 88% increase. Over the same period, below-secondary workers fell from 455,000 to 280,000 (−39%). The labour force is structurally shifting toward high-qualification workers, raising valid questions about whether job creation has matched this pace.

Planned Analysis Methods:

  • Year-on-year growth rate of university share by cohort (pct_change())
    • This field calculates the growth rate of educational attainment by measuring the annual percentage-point increase in university degrees within each band. By applying the pct_change() function, we can identify the specific years where the hike was the steepest as well as Singapore’s university expansion. This allows us to correlate spikes in higher education attainment with shifts such as the increase in university intake capacities during the early 2010s.
  • Cohort-tracking: following the 25–29 group of 2000 as they move into older age bands over successive years
    • This method involves tracking the same birth cohort (e.g. those aged 25–29 in 2000) as they age into subsequent bands (30–34, 35–39, etc.) across different years. By analyzing how the university attainment of this cohort evolves over time, we can confirm whether the observed increase in university share is indeed a cohort effect rather than an age effect.
  • Gender-Education Parity Index: female-to-male university attainment ratio by age group and year
    • This metric introduces a “Gender-Education Parity Index” (GEPI) to quantify the shifting educational landscape across different age bands. By calculating the ratio of female university attainment to male attainment within each age band, we can clearly see the point in which male dominance in tertiary education transitioned to a more balanced and/or female dominance instead . This index portrays how these educational shifts might impact future workforce employment as well as leadership opportunities for each gender.
  • Labour-market absorption rate: ratio of degree-holder population growth to labour force degree-holder growth
    • This analysis compares the growth rate of degree holders in the resident population to the growth rate of degree holders in the labour force. By calculating this ratio, we can assess whether the labour market has been able to absorb the increasing number of degree holders. A ratio close to 1 would suggest that job creation for degree holders has kept pace with their population growth, while a ratio significantly less than 1 could indicate potential underemployment or a mismatch between educational attainment and job opportunities.

7. Planned Work Distribution

Team Member Primary Tasks Est. Hours
Member A Data acquisition, cleaning pipeline (Steps 1–4), reproducibility check ~12 hrs
Member B Feature engineering, dataset integration, cohort-tracking analysis ~12 hrs
Member C Chart 1 (time-series) + Chart 3 (labour force), Quarto setup & theming ~12 hrs
Member D Chart 2 (butterfly redesign), critique write-up, annotation layer ~12 hrs
All Members Revealjs slides, MP4 recording, scripts, peer review ~8 hrs each

Note: Distribution will be adjusted based on implementation complexity. All members are responsible for reviewing one another’s code, visualizations, and written sections before final submission.

8. References

8.1 Article Source

  • D. Yuen, “A Data-Driven Look at Singapore’s ‘Graduate Unemployment’ Debate,” FYT Consultants, Sep. 17, 2025. https://www.fytconsultants.com/single-post/a-data-driven-look-at-singapore-s-graduate-unemployment-debate

8.2 Data Sources

  • Singapore Department of Statistics, 2026, “Singapore Residents Aged 25 Years & Over By Highest Qualification Attained (HQA), Sex And Age Group, Annual,” data.gov.sg. Accessed: February 26, 2026. [Online]. Available: https://data.gov.sg/datasets/d_1020b8ee03923042a8ab8e26eef58093/view

  • Ministry of Manpower, “Labour Force in Singapore 2024,” Mom.gov.sg, 2024. https://stats.mom.gov.sg/Pages/Labour-Force-In-Singapore-2024.aspx

9. Appendix: Data Dictionary

The two tidy objects produced by the data engineering pipeline are documented below. Every column used by the visualization or tooltip layer is defined here with its source, unit, valid range, and role in the chart.

A.1 employment_data — Primary Visualization Table

120 rows · one row per age group × sex × education level combination.

Column R Type Source Unit / Range Role in Chart
age factor (12 levels) B1 & G4 row labels Age band e.g. "25-29" Y-axis position
sex factor (2 levels) B1 & G4 block headers "Male" or "Female" Left / right butterfly wing
education factor (5 levels) G4 column headers See level definitions below Stacked fill colour
emp_rate numeric B1, Sheet B1, col 13 Percent, 0–100 Total bar width
prop numeric Derived: count_k / total_k Proportion, 0–1 Each segment’s share of bar width
count_k numeric G4, education columns Thousands of employed residents Tooltip display
total_k numeric G4, total column Thousands of employed residents Proportion denominator
bar_extent numeric Derived: emp_rate × prop Percent-points, 0–100 Width of each stacked segment
xmin2 numeric Derived: cumulative stacking + sign flip Signed percent, −100 to +100 Left edge of segment (geom_rect)
xmax2 numeric Derived: cumulative stacking + sign flip Signed percent, −100 to +100 Right edge of segment (geom_rect)

Education level factor definitions (in stacking order, bottom to top):

Level Label Description
1 Below Secondary No secondary qualification attained
2 Secondary GCE N/O-Level or equivalent
3 Post-Secondary (Non-Tertiary) ITE, vocational certificates
4 Diploma & Prof Qual Polytechnic diploma or professional qualification
5 Degree Bachelor’s degree or above

Sign convention for xmin2 / xmax2: negative values place the segment on the Male (left) wing; positive values place it on the Female (right) wing. A value of 0 is the central age-group spine.

A.2 emp_pills — Bar-End Label Table

24 rows · one row per age group × sex combination.

Column R Type Source Unit / Range Role in Chart
age factor (12 levels) Derived from employment_data Age band Y-axis alignment
sex factor (2 levels) Derived from employment_data "Male" or "Female" Determines label side
emp_rate numeric B1, Sheet B1 Percent, 0–100 Label text displayed in pill
bar_end numeric Derived: −emp_rate (Male) / +emp_rate (Female) Signed percent X-position of pill label anchor

Note: emp_pills is a summary of employment_data with education collapsed. It drives only the pill annotation layer (geom_text) and is not used for any bar geometry.
